findings.section: ICD-10-CM Burn and Corrosion Coding Guidelines
findings.summary: The older and newer versions present the exact same coding logic regarding the distinction between thermal/electrical burns and chemical corrosions, the classification of current burns by depth and site, and the specific usage rules for categories T30, T31, and T32. The text excerpts provided for both versions are virtually identical, indicating that the guidelines have not been updated or revised between these periods. There are no new clinical criteria, reversed guidance, or expanded scopes introduced in the 2025 version compared to the 2024 version.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Guidelines define burn classification by depth and site with T30 restricted for unspecified regions -> October 2025 (New Version): Guidelines define burn classification by depth and site with T30 restricted for unspecified regions.
October 1, 2024 (Older): T31/T32 used for unspecified sites or mortality data -> October 2025 (New Version): T31/T32 used for unspecified sites or mortality data.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly instructs coders to assign postoperative pain to Chapter 19 and reserve G89.0/G89.4 for specifically documented syndromes. The October 2025 version retains these exact instructions, including the requirement to use Chapter 19 codes for device-related pain and the specific usage of G89.18 or G89.28 as additional codes. There are no substantive alterations to the coding logic, definitions, or behavioral requirements between the two versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assigns postoperative pain to Chapter 19 and reserves G89.0/G89.4 for specific syndromes -> October 2025 (New Version): Assigns postoperative pain to Chapter 19 and reserves G89.0/G89.4 for specific syndromes
October 1, 2024 (Older): Uses Chapter 19 and T codes for device pain with G89.18/28 as additional codes -> October 2025 (New Version): Uses Chapter 19 and T codes for device pain with G89.18/28 as additional codes

findings.summary: The older guideline focused exclusively on prohibiting additional codes for symptoms in general combination codes and specifically for poisonings in categories T36-T65. The newer version retains these rules but significantly expands the scope by introducing mandatory assignment rules for category I13 (Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease), requiring specific sequencing with categories I50 and N18.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Rules limited to general symptom combination codes and categories T36-T65 -> October 2025 (New Version): Added mandatory rules for category I13 assignment when hypertension coexists with heart and chronic kidney disease.
October 1, 2024 (Older): No mention of sequencing requirements for heart failure or kidney disease stages -> October 2025 (New Version): Requires additional code from category I50 for heart failure and secondary code from category N18 for chronic kidney disease stages.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain verbatim identical text regarding the systemic nature of osteoporosis and the exclusion of site components in Category M81. Both guidelines explicitly state that site codes belong to Category M80 and mandate the use of status code Z87.310 for patients with a history of healed fractures. There are no functional differences in the coding rules, definitions, or requirements between the two versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Site is not a component of codes under category M81 for osteoporosis without current pathological fracture. -> October 2025 (New Version): Site is not a component of codes under category M81 for osteoporosis without current pathological fracture.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Status code Z87.310 should follow the M81 code for patients with a history of osteoporosis fractures. -> October 2025 (New Version): Status code Z87.310 should follow the M81 code for patients with a history of osteoporosis fractures.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions present the exact same clinical definitions and coding instructions for hypertensive heart/kidney disease in O10 and bacterial sepsis in P36. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments, such as the removal of specific examples (Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, Enterococcus) in the new version's behavior list and a slight wording shift regarding severe sepsis codes from 'should be used' to 'may be assigned'. These changes do not alter the underlying coding logic or compliance requirements.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Additional codes for severe sepsis (R65.2-) and acute organ dysfunction should be used if applicable. -> October 2025 (New Version): Additional codes for severe sepsis (R65.2-) and acute organ dysfunction may be assigned if applicable.
October 1, 2024 (Older): If the P36 code includes the causal organism, an additional code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus... or B96... should not be assigned. -> October 2025 (New Version): If a P36 code includes the causal organism, additional codes from categories B95 or B96 should not be assigned.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical instructions for assigning ICD-10 codes F68.1- and F68.A for factitious disorder cases. The guidelines for coding victims using T74 or T76 based on confirmed or suspected abuse status are also exactly the same in both texts. There are no logical, clinical, or procedural differences between the two versions; they appear to be duplicates.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code F68.1- for self-imposed cases and F68.A for perpetrator cases -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code F68.1- for self-imposed cases and F68.A for perpetrator cases
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign T74/T76 to victims based on confirmed/suspected abuse -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign T74/T76 to victims based on confirmed/suspected abuse

findings.summary: The older and newer guidelines present the exact same requirements for assigning codes for substance use disorders in remission, relying on provider documentation and clinical judgment. Both versions mandate that mild disorders map to abuse in remission codes while moderate or severe disorders map to dependence in remission codes. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating no update to the coding rules, only a potential metadata or chunking change in the dataset.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Selection of codes requires provider's clinical judgment and is assigned only on the basis of provider documentation. -> October 2025 (New Version): Selection of codes requires provider's clinical judgment and is assigned only on the basis of provider documentation.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present identical logic for sequencing encounter reasons before history codes and defining the criteria for personal versus family history codes. The text excerpts provided for both versions are verbatim identical, indicating no substantive update to the coding rules. Minor variations in the extracted profile summaries (e.g., 'Risk must be linked' vs 'Risk must be established') do not reflect a change in the underlying guideline behavior.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): History codes are acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit. -> October 2025 (New Version): History codes are acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit.
October 1, 2024 (Older): The reason for the encounter (e.g., screening) should be sequenced first. -> October 2025 (New Version): The reason for the encounter (e.g., screening or counseling) should be sequenced first.

findings.section: ICD-10 Coding for Retained Products of Conception
findings.summary: The older and newer guidelines provide identical instructions for assigning O03.4 and O07.4 to subsequent encounters of retained products of conception without complications. Both versions explicitly state that these codes apply even if the patient was previously discharged with a diagnosis of complete abortion. The requirement to assign specific complication codes (O03.-, O04.-, O07.-) instead of the uncomplicated codes remains unchanged in both texts.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign O03.4 for subsequent encounters of incomplete spontaneous abortion without complication. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign O03.4 for incomplete spontaneous abortion without complication in subsequent encounters.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Apply these codes even if the patient was previously discharged with a diagnosis of complete abortion. -> October 2025 (New Version): This advice is appropriate even when the patient was discharged previously with a discharge diagnosis of complete abortion.

findings.section: ICD-10 Category Z05 Newborn Evaluation Coding
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same definition, behaviors, and requirements for assigning Z05 codes to healthy newborns. The raw text excerpts are identical in both versions, indicating that the coding rule has remained static without any updates to the logic or scope.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign a code from category Z05 for healthy newborns evaluated for suspected conditions ruled out -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign a code from category Z05 for healthy newborns evaluated for suspected conditions ruled out
October 1, 2024 (Older): Do not use Z05 if signs or symptoms are documented -> October 2025 (New Version): Do not use Z05 if signs or symptoms are documented

findings.section: ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines for Signs and Symptoms
findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, explicitly states that signs and symptoms routinely associated with a disease process should not be assigned as additional codes. The newer version from October 2025 contains the exact same wording and logic, indicating no substantive update to the coding rule. The only difference is the metadata identifier (Chunk 32 vs Chunk 464), which reflects a database update rather than a change in clinical guidance.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Signs and symptoms that are associated routinely with a disease process should not be assigned as additional codes, unless otherwise instructed by the classification. -> October 2025 (New Version): Signs and symptoms that are associated routinely with a disease process should not be assigned as additional codes, unless otherwise instructed by the classification.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 contain identical text regarding the restrictions on maternal, obstetric, and Chapter 16 codes. There are no differences in the coding logic, requirements, or behavioral rules between the two versions. The text is a verbatim copy, indicating no substantive update occurred for this specific feature.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes in this chapter are never for use on the maternal record. Codes from Chapter 15, the obstetric chapter, are never permitted on the newborn record. Chapter 16 codes may be used throughout the life of the patient if the condition is still present. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes in this chapter are never for use on the maternal record. Codes from Chapter 15, the obstetric chapter, are never permitted on the newborn record. Chapter 16 codes may be used throughout the life of the patient if the condition is still present.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines define Category Z20 and Z77 codes with the exact same criteria for suspected exposure to communicable diseases and hazardous health conditions. The core logic regarding the use of these codes as first-listed or secondary codes remains unchanged between versions. The only variation is a minor addition in the requirements section of the newer version asking providers to document the nature of the contact, which does not alter the fundamental coding logic.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements listed documentation of exposure type and contact method. -> October 2025 (New Version): Requirements added specific instruction for provider to document the nature of the contact or exposure hazard.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same requirements for assigning T86 codes, including the necessity for organ function impact and the use of two codes. The raw text excerpts are character-for-character identical, indicating a copy-paste update rather than a clinical revision. There are no new conditions, definitions, or sequencing rules introduced in the newer version.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes under category T86 are used for both complications and rejection of transplanted organs. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes under category T86 are used for both complications and rejection of transplanted organs.
October 1, 2024 (Older): A transplant complication code is only assigned if the complication affects the function of the transplanted organ. -> October 2025 (New Version): A transplant complication code is only assigned if the complication affects the function of the transplanted organ.

findings.summary: The older version broadly stated that genetic carrier status indicates a person does not have the disease, while the newer version clarifies the specific definition for Z14 as carrying a gene that may be passed to offspring. The newer version also introduces a specific sequencing rule for genetic susceptibility (Z15) based on whether the patient currently has the condition or not, which was not explicitly detailed in the older text. Both versions maintain the core rule that status codes should not be combined with diagnosis codes that already include the status information.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Genetic carrier status indicates the person does not have the disease and is not at risk of developing it. -> October 2025 (New Version): Genetic carrier status (Z14) indicates a person carries a gene associated with a disease that may be passed to offspring, but the person does not currently have the disease.
October 1, 2024 (Older): General instruction on sequencing infection codes for resistance. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specific sequencing rule for genetic susceptibility (Z15) where the current condition is coded first if present, or follow-up is coded first if the condition no longer exists.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions present identical coding protocols for categories T36-T50, including the rule to default to accidental intent when unspecified. Both versions mandate the use of additional codes for all manifestations and require separate documentation for abuse or dependence diagnoses. The text excerpts confirm that the specific examples and classification criteria for drug interactions and prescription errors remain unchanged between the two versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code intent as accidental if unknown or unspecified, and only use undetermined intent if documentation explicitly states it cannot be determined. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code intent as accidental if unknown or unspecified, and only use undetermined intent if documentation explicitly states it cannot be determined.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Classify drug interactions (including with alcohol) and prescription errors resulting in toxicity as poisoning. -> October 2025 (New Version): Classify drug toxicity or reactions resulting from interactions between nonprescribed and correctly prescribed drugs as poisoning.

findings.summary: The older guideline restricted the principal code assignment to Category Z40 generally, while the newer version explicitly expands the scope to include subcategory Z40.8 for encounters involving other prophylactic surgeries. Both versions maintain identical logic regarding the requirement to code concurrent malignancies at different sites and the prohibition against coding Z40.0 for therapeutic removal of existing cancer. The primary delta is the explicit inclusion of Z40.8 in the principal code selection criteria in the 2025 version.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Principal code should be from Category Z40 generally -> October 2025 (New Version): Principal code should be from subcategory Z40.0 or Z40.8 specifically.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Followed by appropriate codes to identify risk factors -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicitly mandates assigning additional codes for associated risk factors if applicable.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same instructions for classifying pressure ulcers using category L89 codes. Both versions mandate documentation of the specific site and stage (1-4, deep tissue pressure injury, unspecified, or unstageable) and allow multiple codes for multiple ulcers. There are no alterations in the coding logic, definitions, or reporting requirements between the two texts.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes in category L89 identify the site and stage of the pressure ulcer. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes in category L89 identify the site and stage of the pressure ulcer.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Classification is based on severity designated by stages 1-4, deep tissue pressure injury, unspecified stage, and unstageable. -> October 2025 (New Version): Classification is based on severity designated by stages 1-4, deep tissue pressure injury, unspecified stage, and unstageable.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present identical text, requirements, and behavioral rules for assigning P07 codes. There are no differences in the clinical criteria, such as the requirement for the patient to be a child or adult rather than a newborn, or the condition affecting current health status. The content remains static between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes from category P07 are for use for a child or adult who was premature or had a low birth weight as a newborn and this is affecting the patient's current health status. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes from category P07 are for use for a child or adult who was premature or had a low birth weight as a newborn and this is affecting the patient's current health status.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, explicitly states that 'completed' weeks refer strictly to full weeks, using the example of 39 weeks and 6 days mapping to the 39-week code. The newer version from October 2025 contains the exact same taxonomy description, extracted profile, and raw text excerpt, indicating no alteration in the coding logic or guidance.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code based on full completed weeks only. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code based on full completed weeks only.

findings.section: ICD-10-CM Factors Influencing Health Status Codes
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same text regarding the purpose of Factors Influencing Health Status Codes. Both versions state that these codes are used for circumstances other than disease or injury recorded as diagnoses or problems. There are no alterations in the coding logic, requirements, or behavioral instructions between the two versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes are provided to deal with encounters for circumstances other than a disease or injury. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes are provided to deal with encounters for circumstances other than a disease or injury.
October 1, 2024 (Older): The circumstance must be recorded as a diagnosis or problem. -> October 2025 (New Version): The circumstance must be recorded as a diagnosis or problem.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions present the exact same coding logic for assigning Z codes to explain reasons for healthcare encounters, including transfers and limited history. Both versions explicitly list the same set of specific Z codes (Z59.0, Z59.1, Z59.5, Z75.1, Z75.3, Z75.4, Z76.2, Z99.12) as examples and maintain the rule that these codes are not an all-inclusive list. The only differences are minor formatting variations in the extracted text, such as the removal of the trailing sentence fragment in the new version and slight phrasing adjustments in the 'Behaviors' list, but the underlying clinical guidance remains unchanged.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): The raw text excerpt includes a truncated sentence ending in 'Assign as many codes as necess' -> October 2025 (New Version): The raw text excerpt includes the same truncated sentence ending in 'Assign as many codes as necess'.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text regarding the assignment of remission codes for leukemia and multiple myeloma. Both guidelines explicitly state the same codes (Z85.6, Z85.79) and the same requirement to query providers if documentation is unclear. There are no differences in coding logic, scope, or definitions between the two versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Identical text to New Version -> October 2025 (New Version): Identical text to Old Version
October 1, 2024 (Older): No change in coding rules -> October 2025 (New Version): No change in coding rules

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain identical text regarding the assignment of code E10.A- for early-stage Type 1 diabetes predating symptoms. There are no alterations to the clinical criteria, code selection logic, or documentation requirements between the two versions. The update appears to be a maintenance release with no substantive changes to the coding behavior.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes E10.A- are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes that predates the onset of symptoms. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes E10.A- are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes that predates the onset of symptoms.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present identical instructions for assigning ICD-10 codes H54.3, H54.6-, and H54.7 based on the presence or absence of specific visual impairment categories and laterality information. There are no alterations to the clinical definitions, coding requirements, or behavioral rules between the two versions. The text excerpts provided for both versions are verbatim identical, indicating a copy-paste update rather than a substantive revision.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code H54.3 if blindness or low vision of both eyes is documented without a specific visual impairment category. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code H54.3 if blindness or low vision of both eyes is documented without a specific visual impairment category.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign a code from H54.6- if blindness or low vision in one eye is documented without a specific visual impairment category. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign a code from H54.6- if blindness or low vision in one eye is documented without a specific visual impairment category.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present identical rules for coding fractures, including defaults for unspecified open/closed or displaced/non-displaced status and 7th character usage. While the 2025 version includes a slightly more explicit prohibition on separate bone codes for physeal fractures in the requirements list, the core coding behaviors and logic remain unchanged between the two versions. The text excerpts provided for both versions are also identical, confirming no substantive update occurred.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Fractures not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed. -> October 2025 (New Version): Fractures not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed.
October 1, 2024 (Older): A code from category M80 should be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture. -> October 2025 (New Version): A code from category M80 must be used instead of a traumatic fracture code for patients with known osteoporosis who suffer a fracture from minor trauma that would not typically break a healthy bone.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain identical text regarding CKD staging (N18.2, N18.3x, N18.4) and the mandatory assignment of N18.6 for ESRD. There are no alterations to the clinical criteria, code assignments, or the logic for handling concurrent documentation of CKD stages and end-stage renal disease. The only variation is the inclusion of the tuberculosis 'and' interpretation rule in the taxonomy description, which is present in both versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): CKD stages 1-5 defined with N18.6 precedence for ESRD -> October 2025 (New Version): CKD stages 1-5 defined with N18.6 precedence for ESRD
October 1, 2024 (Older): Word 'and' interpreted as 'and' or 'or' for TB classification -> October 2025 (New Version): Word 'and' interpreted as 'and' or 'or' for TB classification

findings.summary: The older version explicitly restricted the use of category I22 to subsequent Type 1 or unspecified AMIs, while the newer version clarifies that Type 2 AMIs must always be coded as I21.A1 regardless of whether they are described as NSTEMI or STEMI. The newer version also adds a specific prohibition against using code I24.89 for demand ischemia in Type 2 AMIs, whereas the older version only mentioned avoiding I24.8 generally. Both versions maintain the rule that subsequent Type 4 or 5 AMIs are coded as I21.A9, but the newer text provides more granular instruction on the clinical documentation required for Type 2 classification.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Subsequent type 2 AMI must be coded as I21.A1 with underlying cause coded first if applicable. -> October 2025 (New Version): If a Type 2 AMI is described as NSTEMI or STEMI, only assign code I21.A1.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code I24.8 must not be assigned for demand ischemia. -> October 2025 (New Version): Do not assign code I24.89, Other forms of acute ischemic heart disease, for the demand ischemia.

findings.summary: The older version established the general rule that codes must be used at the highest level of specificity and that three-character codes are invalid if subdivided. The newer version retains these core rules but adds a critical clarification that only specific codes are permissible for reporting, explicitly excluding categories and subcategories from being used as standalone codes.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Mandates use of codes at highest specificity and invalidates codes not fully expanded -> October 2025 (New Version): Adds explicit prohibition on using categories or subcategories for reporting, requiring only specific codes.
October 1, 2024 (Older): States a three-character code is invalid if not subdivided -> October 2025 (New Version): Reiterates the invalidity of three-character codes if subdivided while reinforcing the requirement for fourth, fifth, and sixth characters if the category allows subdivision.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text regarding the assignment of ICD-10-CM codes I21.0-I21.4 for Type 1 AMI. Both versions explicitly state that if a Type 1 NSTEMI evolves to STEMI, the STEMI code is assigned, and if a STEMI converts to NSTEMI due to thrombolytic therapy, it remains coded as STEMI. The 4-week reporting window and the requirement for the 'other diagnoses' definition are also unchanged between the two texts.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Subcategories I21.0-I21.2 and I21.3 are used for type 1 STEMI; I21.4 is used for type 1 NSTEMI -> October 2025 (New Version): Subcategories I21.0-I21.2 and I21.3 are used for type 1 STEMI; I21.4 is used for type 1 NSTEMI
October 1, 2024 (Older): If a type 1 NSTEMI evolves to STEMI, assign the STEMI code -> October 2025 (New Version): If a type 1 NSTEMI evolves to STEMI, assign the STEMI code

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain identical text regarding the definition of Excludes1 notes as 'pure excludes' and the rule that excluded codes cannot be used simultaneously with the primary code. Both versions explicitly detail the exception for unrelated conditions and provide the exact same clinical example involving code F45.8 and G47.63 to illustrate when unrelated conditions can be coded together. The logic for querying providers when the relationship between conditions is unclear remains unchanged between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Identical logic and example text -> October 2025 (New Version): Identical logic and example text

findings.section: ICD-10 Code Subdivision and Validation Rules
findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain the exact same raw text excerpt regarding three-character code usage and full character requirements. There are no substantive differences in the coding logic, definitions, or validation criteria between the two versions. The extracted profiles and taxonomy descriptions are functionally identical, indicating a copy-paste update rather than a guideline revision.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): A three-character code is to be used only if it is not further subdivided. A code is invalid if it has not been coded to the full number of characters required for that code, including the 7 th character, if applicable. -> October 2025 (New Version): A three-character code is to be used only if it is not further subdivided. A code is invalid if it has not been coded to the full number of characters required for that code, including the 7 th character, if applicable.

findings.section: ICD-10 Code Inclusion and Index Assignment
findings.summary: The core coding logic remains identical between the October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions, as the raw text excerpts are verbatim copies. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments in the extracted profile, such as the addition of 'explicitly' to the requirements and slight rephrasing of the taxonomy description, without altering the actual assignment rules.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Terms must be included under a code to be used for that condition. -> October 2025 (New Version): Terms must be explicitly listed under a code to be considered inclusion terms.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assignment is permitted for additional terms found only in the Alphabetic Index. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assignment is permitted for terms found exclusively in the Alphabetic Index.

findings.summary: The older version explicitly stated that the 7th character 'S' is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code, while the newer version clarifies the sequencing order by stating the specific type of sequela is sequenced first, followed by the injury code. This represents a shift from a restriction on code assignment to a directive on code sequencing logic. Both versions maintain the core rule that active treatment status, rather than provider history, determines the character assignment.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): 7th character 'S' identifies the injury responsible for a sequela and is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code. -> October 2025 (New Version): 7th character 'S' identifies the injury responsible for a sequela and is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code.

findings.section: ICD Code Designation and NEC Index Logic
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline states that codes titled 'other' or 'other specified' are used when the medical record provides detail for which a specific code does not exist, and that Alphabetic Index entries with NEC designate these codes. The October 2025 version contains the exact same text and logic regarding the use of NEC entries to represent specific disease entities lacking a dedicated code. There are no alterations in the coding rules, requirements, or behavioral expectations between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes titled 'other' or 'other specified' are for use when the information in the medical record provides detail for which a specific code does not exist. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes titled 'other' or 'other specified' are for use when the information in the medical record provides detail for which a specific code does not exist.

findings.summary: The text provided for both the October 1, 2024 version and the October 2025 version is character-for-character identical, containing no updates to the coding logic or instructional notes. The behavior regarding conventions serving as general rules independent of specific guidelines remains exactly the same in both versions. Consequently, there are no functional differences to analyze between the two excerpts.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): The conventions are the general rules for use of the classification independent of the guidelines. -> October 2025 (New Version): The conventions are the general rules for use of the classification independent of the guidelines.

findings.summary: The older version (October 1, 2024) and the newer version (October 2025) contain identical text regarding the sequencing of atherosclerotic heart disease with angina pectoris. Both versions state that combination codes include both conditions, making additional codes unnecessary, and that a causal relationship is assumed unless documented otherwise. Furthermore, both versions explicitly require that if a patient is admitted for acute myocardial infarction (AMI), the AMI must be sequenced before the coronary artery disease. There are no behavioral differences, only a change in the metadata identifier (Chunk 177 vs Chunk 605).
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Combination codes for atherosclerotic heart disease with angina pectoris include both conditions, making an additional code for angina unnecessary. -> October 2025 (New Version): Combination codes for atherosclerotic heart disease with angina pectoris include both conditions, making an additional code for angina unnecessary.
October 1, 2024 (Older): A causal relationship can be assumed in a patient with both atherosclerosis and angina pectoris, unless the documentation indicates the angina is due to something other than the atherosclerosis. -> October 2025 (New Version): A causal relationship can be assumed in a patient with both atherosclerosis and angina pectoris, unless the documentation indicates the angina is due to something other than the atherosclerosis.
October 1, 2024 (Older): If a patient with coronary artery disease is admitted due to an acute myocardial infarction (AMI), the AMI should be sequenced before the coronary artery disease. -> October 2025 (New Version): If a patient with coronary artery disease is admitted due to an acute myocardial infarction (AMI), the AMI should be sequenced before the coronary artery disease.

findings.summary: The core definitions for brackets, parentheses, and colons remain identical between the October 1, 2024 version and the October 2025 version. While the 2025 version includes additional notes on 'Not elsewhere classifiable' and commas, the primary coding logic for syntax symbols has not been altered. The text excerpts provided for both versions confirm that the rules for nonessential modifiers and subentry precedence are unchanged.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Includes detailed examples of nonessential modifiers vs subentries in the Enteritis case. -> October 2025 (New Version): Retains the exact same Enteritis example but adds definitions for 'Not elsewhere classifiable' and 'Not otherwise specified'.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 present the exact same definition regarding the 3-to-7 character hierarchy. Both texts explicitly state that three-character codes serve as category headings and are subdivided by subsequent characters to provide specificity. There are no logical, procedural, or clinical differences between the two excerpts; the content is functionally identical.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes are composed of 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 characters with three-character codes serving as the heading of a category. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes are composed of 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 characters with three-character codes serving as the heading of a category.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same procedural steps for code selection, including the mandatory verification in the Tabular List and the interpretation of dashes. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating that the underlying coding logic has not been altered between these versions. Any differences are purely cosmetic or related to metadata rather than the operational workflow itself.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Locate term in Alphabetic Index, verify in Tabular List, read notations, select full code in Tabular List -> October 2025 (New Version): Locate term in Alphabetic Index, verify in Tabular List, read notations, select full code in Tabular List
October 1, 2024 (Older): Dash indicates additional characters required; verify Tabular List for 7th character even if dash absent -> October 2025 (New Version): Dash indicates additional characters required; verify Tabular List for 7th character even if dash absent

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 version and the October 2025 version contain virtually identical text regarding the interpretation of relational terms like 'with' and 'in' as presuming a causal relationship. Both versions mandate coding conditions as related without explicit provider documentation unless the documentation explicitly states they are unrelated or a specific guideline (like sepsis) requires documented linkage. The only differences are minor formatting artifacts in the raw text excerpts, such as line breaks and a fragmented sentence structure in the new version, which do not alter the coding logic.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): The text flows as a complete paragraph regarding the sepsis guideline example. -> October 2025 (New Version): The text contains a line break interrupting the sentence 'specifically requires a documented linkage between two conditions (e.g., sepsis guideline for 'acute organ dysfunction that is not clearly associated with the sepsis'). documented, provider documentation must link the conditions in order to code them as related.'
October 1, 2024 (Older): The final sentence regarding alphabetical sequencing is complete. -> October 2025 (New Version): The final sentence is identical in content but appears after the fragmented previous sentence due to formatting changes.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same validation logic for 7th character placement and the use of placeholder 'X' characters. There are no alterations in the requirements, such as the mandate for codes to be exactly 6 characters before appending the 7th, or the instruction to fill empty positions with 'X'. The text excerpts and extracted behaviors are verbatim identical, indicating a copy-paste update rather than a policy revision.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Validates 7th character position and 'X' placeholder usage -> October 2025 (New Version): Validates 7th character position and 'X' placeholder usage (identical logic)
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requires codes to be 6 characters before appending 7th -> October 2025 (New Version): Requires codes to be 6 characters before appending 7th (identical logic)

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain verbatim identical text regarding the structure of categories, subcategories, and codes. There are no alterations to the coding logic, character requirements, or hierarchical definitions between the two versions. The only differences are minor formatting artifacts in the extracted profile summaries, not the source content itself.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Categories are strictly 3 characters and may have no further subdivision. -> October 2025 (New Version): Categories are always 3 characters and may have no further subdivision.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Final codes may be 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 characters. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes may be 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 characters.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline establishes the requirement for the 'X' character in categories T36-T50 to ensure code validity and support future expansion. The October 2025 version retains this exact logic, merely adding context about legacy system parsing without altering the core coding rule. Both versions explicitly state that failure to use the placeholder renders the code invalid, indicating no substantive shift in clinical or administrative requirements.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): The 'X' acts as a structural delimiter to ensure the code maintains a consistent seven-character length. -> October 2025 (New Version): The 'X' acts as a structural delimiter that ensures the code string maintains a consistent length and format, which is critical for automated parsing and sorting algorithms.

findings.summary: The core definition stating that excluded codes are independent remains identical in both versions. While the newer version introduces specific terminology ('Not Included' and 'Rule Out') to describe the relationships, the fundamental logic and the introductory text regarding independence are unchanged. The behavioral outcome for coders regarding the independence of codes has not shifted.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): One type is mandatory, requiring a choice between codes; the other is informational, allowing both codes if clinically appropriate. -> October 2025 (New Version): One type represents a 'Not Included' relationship where the excluded code is a subset; the other represents a 'Rule Out' relationship where codes are mutually exclusive conditions.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same definition, requirements, and raw text excerpt for default code assignment. There are no alterations to the logic, scope, or clinical criteria between the two versions. The text is a verbatim repetition, indicating that the coding rule remained static during this update cycle.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): A code listed next to a main term is a default code assigned when no additional info exists -> October 2025 (New Version): A code listed next to a main term is a default code assigned when no additional info exists
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements state condition must be documented and no specific info present -> October 2025 (New Version): Requirements state condition must be documented and no specific info present

findings.section: Symptom and Sign Code Reporting Guidelines
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain identical text regarding the use of Chapter 18 codes (R00-R99) for symptoms when a diagnosis is not established. There are no substantive differences in the coding logic, requirements, or behavioral instructions between the two versions. The only variations are minor formatting inconsistencies in the provided text excerpts, such as punctuation and capitalization, which do not alter the clinical guidance.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Text excerpt uses 'R00.0 - R99' with spaces and hyphens -> October 2025 (New Version): Text excerpt uses 'R00.0 - R99' with identical formatting (note: actual logic is identical).
October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text excerpt contains 'contain many, but not all codes' -> October 2025 (New Version): Raw text excerpt contains 'contains many, but not all, codes' (minor grammatical variation only).

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same clinical logic regarding the sequencing of underlying etiology codes before manifestation codes. Both versions explicitly state that codes titled 'in diseases classified elsewhere' cannot be used as principal diagnoses and must follow the etiology code. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating no substantive update to the coding convention.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Underlying etiology conditions must be sequenced first, followed by manifestation codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): Underlying etiology codes must be sequenced first, followed by manifestation codes when a combination exists.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 version explicitly required referencing the histological term in the Alphabetic Index before the Neoplasm Table if documented. The October 2025 version clarifies this by stating the combination of overlapping sites must not be specifically indexed elsewhere before using the .8 code, refining the sequencing logic for neoplasms.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): The histological term must be referenced in the Alphabetic Index before the Neoplasm Table if documented. -> October 2025 (New Version): The combination of overlapping sites must not be specifically indexed elsewhere before using the .8 code.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Guidelines focus on general structure and principal diagnosis selection. -> October 2025 (New Version): Guidelines are explicitly organized into four specific sections: Section I (structure/conventions), Section II (principal diagnosis for non-outpatient), Section III (additional diagnoses for non-outpatient), and Section IV (outpatient coding).
October 1, 2024 (Older): The term 'encounter' is not explicitly defined in the provided text. -> October 2025 (New Version): The term 'encounter' encompasses all healthcare settings, including hospital admissions.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text for the feature behaviors, requirements, and raw excerpts, indicating no substantive update to the coding logic. The taxonomy descriptions are also semantically equivalent, merely rephrasing the same constraints on single-code reporting and documentation specificity. Consequently, the operational rules for assigning diagnosis codes remain exactly the same between the two versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Each unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code may be reported only once for an encounter. -> October 2025 (New Version): Each unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code may be reported only once for an encounter.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Documentation should describe the patient's condition using terminology which includes specific diagnoses as well as symptoms, problems, or reasons for the encounter. -> October 2025 (New Version): Documentation should describe the patient's condition using terminology which includes specific diagnoses as well as symptoms, problems, or reasons for the encounter.

findings.summary: The older version explicitly detailed rules for coding borderline diagnoses (treating them as confirmed unless a specific index entry exists) and mandated queries for unclear documentation. The newer version removes these specific borderline instructions entirely, focusing solely on the general rule of listing the chief reason for encounter first. While the core logic for principal diagnosis selection remains identical in both texts, the removal of the specific borderline guidance represents a reduction in scope rather than a logical contradiction or expansion.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Borderline diagnoses are coded as confirmed unless a specific index entry exists, and coders are encouraged to query for clarification. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specific instructions regarding borderline diagnoses and the requirement to query for clarification are omitted.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicitly states no distinction is made between inpatient and outpatient settings for borderline conditions. -> October 2025 (New Version): Does not mention care setting distinctions or borderline condition specifics.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain the exact same raw text regarding Z02 guidelines, including rules for administrative exams, suspected conditions, and abnormal findings. The extracted profiles and requirements lists are semantically identical, indicating no actual change in coding logic occurred between these versions. The only difference is the metadata label (Taxonomy description) which was slightly updated to be more concise in the new version.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Taxonomy description mentions specific ICD-10-CM code mappings for Z02.9 and Z13.9 -> October 2025 (New Version): Taxonomy description is generalized to define rules for Z02 routine examinations without specific code examples.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text excerpt ends abruptly mid-sentence ('specific abnormal find') -> October 2025 (New Version): Raw text excerpt ends abruptly mid-sentence ('specific abnormal find').

findings.summary: The core coding logic remains identical in both versions, as the raw text excerpts are verbatim copies of each other. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments in the taxonomy descriptions and the JSON structure of the code examples (e.g., changing from a list of strings to a key-value object), which do not alter the clinical instructions. The requirements for sequencing, the distinction from aftercare codes, and the handling of recurrence are unchanged.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Taxonomy description listed Z08 and Z09 as separate string entries in a list format -> October 2025 (New Version): Taxonomy description presents Z08 and Z09 as a key-value object mapping the code to its description.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text excerpt included a specific list of Z code categories as strings -> October 2025 (New Version): Raw text excerpt restructured the Z code categories into a JSON object format.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements section explicitly listed 'History code must be assigned to describe the healed condition and its treatment' -> October 2025 (New Version): This specific requirement was removed from the list, though the instruction remains in the behaviors and raw text.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Behaviors section included 'Follow-up codes may be used to explain multiple visits' -> October 2025 (New Version): This specific behavior was removed from the list, though the instruction remains in the raw text.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions present the exact same logic for assigning POA indicators, including the handling of exempt codes, explicit documentation, and uncertain diagnoses. The only differences are minor phrasing adjustments in the requirements section, such as changing 'Query the provider' to 'Coders are encouraged to query providers' and 'uncertain diagnoses' to 'uncertain cases', without altering the underlying coding rules. The core behaviors regarding assigning 'Y', 'N', 'U', 'W', or leaving the field blank remain functionally identical across both versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Query the provider when documentation is unclear to avoid routine assignment of 'U'. -> October 2025 (New Version): Coders are encouraged to query providers when documentation is unclear before assigning 'U'.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'Y' for uncertain diagnoses. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign 'Y' for uncertain cases.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 version and the October 2025 version present the exact same coding logic for Section 21, focusing on assigning codes for factors influencing health status and screening results when no confirmed diagnosis exists. The specific behaviors regarding distinguishing normal findings from abnormal results requiring investigation are consistent across both versions. There are no substantive differences in the coding rules, requirements, or clinical definitions between the two texts.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code factors influencing health status and contact with health services when documented as the reason for encounter | Assign screening codes when a patient undergoes a screening test or procedure for a specific condition -> October 2025 (New Version): Factors influencing health status and contact with health services are coded under Section I.C.21. | Screening codes are utilized to document preventive health services and diagnostic tests.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Condition must be documented as a factor influencing health status or contact with health services | Screening must be performed for a specific condition and documented as such -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes must be assigned based on documentation of factors influencing health status. | Screening codes require documentation of the specific test or procedure performed.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline established the rule that 'Y' is assigned if all conditions in a single code were present on admission, while 'N' is assigned if any were not. The October 2025 version retains this exact logic without altering the criteria, examples, or requirements. The only variations between the two texts are minor formatting adjustments, such as the use of commas in the introductory clause and the removal of a period before the final example.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Uses 'e.g.' without a preceding comma in the introductory clause and ends the excerpt with a period after the example. -> October 2025 (New Version): Uses 'e.g.,' with a preceding comma in the introductory clause and omits the final period after the example.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text regarding the classification of dementia categories F01, F02, and F03 based on etiology and severity. Both versions mandate assigning the highest severity code if a patient's condition progresses during an inpatient stay and require provider documentation for severity levels. There are no substantive differences in the coding logic, requirements, or behavioral instructions between the two excerpts.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Classify dementia (categories F01, F02, and F03) based on etiology and severity. -> October 2025 (New Version): Classify dementia (categories F01, F02, and F03) based on etiology and severity.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, explicitly states that coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses do not apply to outpatients and that the UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis does not apply. The newer version from October 2025 retains these exact constraints, stating that inconclusive diagnoses are not coded for outpatients and the UHDDS definition is excluded from outpatient settings. The raw text excerpts provided for both versions are identical, indicating no substantive update to the coding logic or scope.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out) do not apply to outpatients. -> October 2025 (New Version): Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out) do not apply to outpatients.
October 1, 2024 (Older): The UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits. -> October 2025 (New Version): The UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services or provider-based office visits.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly instructs coders to assign U07.1 as the principal diagnosis and J96.0- as an additional code for acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19. The October 2025 version retains this exact logic, merely reformatting the text to remove the phrase 'The following conditions are examples of common respiratory manifestations of COVID-19' before the specific instruction. The clinical requirement and code assignment rules remain identical between the two versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Includes introductory text listing examples of respiratory manifestations before the specific instruction for acute respiratory failure. -> October 2025 (New Version): Removes the introductory list of examples and presents the acute respiratory failure instruction immediately.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Uses the phrase 'assign code(s) for the respiratory manifestation(s) as additional diagnoses' in the general rule. -> October 2025 (New Version): Uses the same phrase but omits the specific introductory context regarding the list of examples.

findings.summary: The older version required a positive test result to confirm an asymptomatic infection or establish a definitive diagnosis for coding purposes. The newer version revises this definition by stating that a provider's documentation of a confirmed diagnosis is sufficient for code U07.1, explicitly removing the requirement for a positive test result. Conversely, the newer version clarifies that terms like 'suspected' or 'possible' strictly preclude the use of U07.1, mandating symptom codes instead, whereas the older version focused more on the positive test confirmation for asymptomatic cases.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Asymptomatic individuals must have a positive test result to be considered infected and assigned the appropriate code. -> October 2025 (New Version): A confirmed diagnosis is assigned based on provider documentation alone, without requiring a positive test result.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Did not explicitly list 'suspected' or 'possible' as exclusions for U07.1 in the main text. -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicitly states that 'suspected,' 'possible,' 'probable,' or 'inconclusive' diagnoses preclude assignment of U07.1 and require symptom coding.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly defined the coding logic for follow-up visits after resolved infections, requiring negative test results and the absence of residual symptoms to assign codes Z09 and Z86.16. The October 2025 version narrows the scope significantly, removing the detailed instructions for post-resolution follow-up evaluations and focusing solely on the assignment of Z11.52 for screening and preoperative testing. This represents a reduction in the specific behavioral rules for the 'Follow-Up Evaluation' aspect of the feature within the provided text.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assigns codes Z09 and Z86.16 for follow-up visits after resolved COVID-19 infections without residual symptoms and negative test results. -> October 2025 (New Version): Does not include instructions for follow-up visits; focuses only on screening assignments.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Directs cases with related symptoms to specific guidelines for malignant neoplasm or other conditions. -> October 2025 (New Version): Omits any reference to symptom management or related condition guidelines.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions present the exact same coding logic for assigning U09.9 for sequelae and prohibiting its use for active infections. The raw text excerpts are identical in both versions, indicating no substantive update to the clinical criteria or coding requirements. The only variation is the inclusion of a separate rule for ARDS (U07.1 and J80) in the older version's taxonomy description, which is not present in the newer version's specific excerpt, but the core feature regarding post-COVID-19 conditions remains unchanged.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Includes specific instruction to assign U07.1 and J80 for ARDS due to COVID-19 in the taxonomy description -> October 2025 (New Version): Omits the ARDS-specific instruction in the provided excerpt, focusing solely on post-COVID-19 sequelae rules.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline defines code O94 for sequelae of initial pregnancy complications requiring future care. The October 2025 version retains this exact definition for O94, despite adding new rules for ARDS (U07.1/J80) in the same text block. The core logic for O94 implementation remains identical between the two versions, with no alteration to the criteria or application of the code.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code O94 is used when an initial complication of pregnancy develops a sequela requiring future care or treatment. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code O94 is used when an initial complication of pregnancy develops a sequela requiring future care or treatment.

findings.summary: The core coding instruction remains identical in both versions, directing the assignment of Z01.84 for antibody testing that is neither confirmatory nor a follow-up after resolution. The only variation is a minor wording adjustment in the requirements section, changing 'Documentation must clarify the purpose of the antibody testing' to 'Documentation must clarify the purpose of the encounter regarding current infection status', which does not alter the clinical logic.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Documentation must clarify the purpose of the antibody testing -> October 2025 (New Version): Documentation must clarify the purpose of the encounter regarding current infection status

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, instructs coders to assign U07.1 and J20.8 for confirmed cases and U07.1 and J40 for NOS cases. The newer version from October 2025 repeats these exact instructions without altering the clinical criteria or code selection logic. The text is effectively identical in content and intent, indicating no substantive update to the coding guidelines.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign U07.1 and J20.8 for confirmed cases, U07.1 and J40 for NOS -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign U07.1 and J20.8 for confirmed cases, U07.1 and J40 for NOS
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requires confirmation of COVID-19 for specific coding -> October 2025 (New Version): Requires confirmation of COVID-19 for specific coding

findings.section: ICD-10 Fall Investigation and Risk Coding
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same definitions and assignment rules for codes R29.6 and Z91.81. The raw text excerpts are identical in both versions, indicating no substantive update to the coding criteria. Minor variations in the extracted profile summaries (e.g., 'recently fallen' vs 'fall to be recent') do not alter the underlying clinical logic or requirements.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code R29.6 requires the encounter to be for investigating the reason for a recent fall. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code R29.6 requires the fall to be recent and the reason to be under investigation.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, mandates the use of codes from ranges A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, and U00-U85 to identify diagnoses and symptoms. The newer version from October 2025 repeats this exact instruction with identical wording and code ranges, indicating no substantive update to the coding logic.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Use appropriate codes from A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, and U00-U85 to identify diagnoses, symptoms, conditions, problems, complaints, or other reasons for the encounter/visit. -> October 2025 (New Version): Use appropriate codes from A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, and U00-U85 to identify diagnoses, symptoms, conditions, problems, complaints, or other reasons for the encounter/visit.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same coding logic for Code Z23, requiring procedure codes to specify the administration and type of immunization. The text excerpts are nearly identical, with only minor phrasing adjustments in the extracted profile (e.g., 'specific type(s)' vs 'type(s)') that do not alter the clinical requirement. There is no substantive shift in how the code is applied or the conditions under which it is valid.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Procedure codes must specify the type(s) of immunizations given. -> October 2025 (New Version): Procedure codes must identify the specific type(s) of immunizations given.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code Z23 may be used as a secondary code if the inoculation is given as a routine part of preventive health care. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code Z23 may be used as a secondary code if the inoculation is given as a routine part of preventive health care.

findings.summary: The core coding logic for assigning U07.1, M35.81, U09.9, and Z20.822 remains identical between the October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions. The only variation is the removal of the qualifier 'confirmed' in the requirement for assigning M35.81 in the newer version, which does not alter the practical application of the guideline. The raw text excerpts are virtually identical, confirming that the operational rules have not shifted.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): MIS diagnosis must be confirmed or documented as suspected to assign M35.81 -> October 2025 (New Version): MIS diagnosis must be documented to assign M35.81
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements explicitly list 'Current COVID-19 infection must be present' -> October 2025 (New Version): Requirements state 'Current COVID-19 infection must be documented'

findings.section: SIRS Coding Guidelines for Noninfectious Origins
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same clinical criteria for assigning R65.10 and R65.11 codes when SIRS is caused by noninfectious conditions. The instruction to assign the underlying condition code first, followed by the SIRS code, remains unchanged. Additionally, the requirement to query providers when the association between acute organ dysfunction and SIRS is unclear is identical in both versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code for underlying noninfectious condition followed by R65.10 or R65.11 when SIRS is documented with no subsequent infection. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code for underlying noninfectious condition followed by R65.10 or R65.11 when SIRS is documented with a noninfectious cause and no subsequent infection.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Query the provider if acute organ dysfunction cannot be determined to be associated with SIRS or due to another condition. -> October 2025 (New Version): Query the provider if acute organ dysfunction is documented but its association with SIRS versus another condition cannot be determined.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain identical text regarding the assignment of code Z20.822 for both asymptomatic and symptomatic individuals with actual or suspected exposure where infection is ruled out. The requirements for confirming infection via guideline I.C.1.g.1.a remain unchanged in both versions. There are no differences in the clinical criteria, code assignments, or referenced guidelines between the two texts.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assigns Z20.822 for asymptomatic/symptomatic exposure with ruled-out infection -> October 2025 (New Version): Assigns Z20.822 for asymptomatic/symptomatic exposure with ruled-out infection
October 1, 2024 (Older): References guideline I.C.21.c.1 for Z20 guidance -> October 2025 (New Version): References guideline I.C.21.c.1 for Z20 guidance

findings.summary: The coding logic for assigning Z28.310 for unvaccinated patients and Z28.311 for partially vaccinated patients remains identical in both versions. The requirements regarding CDC alignment and documentation by clinicians other than the provider are unchanged. The only difference is a minor URL modification in the raw text excerpt, which does not alter the coding behavior.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): CDC website link is https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/ -> October 2025 (New Version): CDC website link is https://www.cdc.gov/covidschedule

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, instructs coders to assign code U07.1 as the principal diagnosis when the encounter is for a nonrespiratory manifestation like viral enteritis. The newer version from October 2025 contains the exact same instruction and raw text excerpt, indicating no alteration in the coding rule. Both guidelines require specific manifestation codes to be listed as additional diagnoses alongside U07.1.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code U07.1 as the principal/first-listed diagnosis for nonrespiratory manifestations -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code U07.1 as the principal/first-listed diagnosis for nonrespiratory manifestations
October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text specifies U07.1 as principal for nonrespiratory manifestations -> October 2025 (New Version): Raw text specifies U07.1 as principal for nonrespiratory manifestations

findings.summary: The older version (October 1, 2024) defines an exception allowing code U07.1 for active confirmed COVID-19 diagnoses based on provider documentation alone, without a positive test. The newer version (October 2025) introduces a separate requirement to assign code Z86.16 for patients with a documented history of the disease. These are two different coding rules for different clinical states (active vs. history) rather than a modification of the same rule.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Establishes an exception to allow code U07.1 for confirmed active COVID-19 diagnoses based on provider documentation without a positive test result. -> October 2025 (New Version): Establishes a requirement to assign code Z86.16 for patients with a documented history of COVID-19.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicitly prohibits using U07.1 for 'suspected', 'possible', 'probable', or 'inconclusive' diagnoses, directing coders to use signs and symptoms. -> October 2025 (New Version): Does not address the coding of uncertain active diagnoses, focusing solely on the history code assignment.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly states that conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone must be coded to the bone site, not the joint, even if the portion is at the joint. The October 2025 version contains the exact same logic and wording, reiterating that the site designation remains the bone regardless of joint involvement. There are no substantive differences in the coding logic, examples, or requirements between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone must be coded to the bone site, not the joint. -> October 2025 (New Version): Conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone must be coded to the bone site, not the joint.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same logic for assigning site and laterality codes in Chapter 13. Both versions mandate that codes represent specific bones, joints, or muscles and provide identical instructions for handling multiple sites versus single multiple-site codes. The only differences are minor phrasing adjustments in the requirements section, such as changing 'must specify' to 'Code must specify', without altering the underlying coding logic.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): The diagnosis must specify the bone, joint, or muscle involved to assign the correct site code. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code must specify the anatomical site (bone, joint, or muscle)
October 1, 2024 (Older): Multiple codes must be used to indicate different sites involved when no multiple site code is provided. -> October 2025 (New Version): Laterality must be assigned if designated in the code set

findings.summary: The older and newer versions both direct coders to Section I.C.21 for factors influencing health status and contact with health services within the Aftercare domain. The requirements remain strictly identical, mandating that diagnoses fall under this specific category and be classified as Aftercare. There are no new conditions, definitions, or rule clarifications introduced in the 2025 version compared to the 2024 version.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Factors are categorized under Aftercare per Section I.C.21 -> October 2025 (New Version): Factors are categorized under Aftercare per Section I.C.21
October 1, 2024 (Older): Diagnosis must fall under factors influencing health status and contact with health services -> October 2025 (New Version): Diagnosis must fall under factors influencing health status and contact with health services

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain identical text excerpts and behavioral definitions for assigning bilateral versus unilateral codes. The guidelines regarding BIA-ALCL coding (C84.7A/C84.7B) and the prohibition of Chapter 19 complication codes are unchanged between the two versions. The only variation is a minor capitalization difference in the phrase 'chapter 19' versus 'Chapter 19', which does not alter the coding logic.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Prohibits complication codes from Chapter 19 for BIA-ALCL -> October 2025 (New Version): Prohibits complication codes from chapter 19 for BIA-ALCL (minor capitalization change only)
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requires bilateral codes if condition exists on both sides -> October 2025 (New Version): Requires bilateral codes if condition exists on both sides (identical logic)

findings.section: ICD-10 Code Assignment for MSSA/MRSA Colonization
findings.summary: The older and newer guidelines present identical logic for assigning codes Z22.322 and Z22.321 based on explicit documentation of carrier status. The core requirement that colonization represents the presence of bacteria without necessarily causing illness remains unchanged in both versions. While the newer version slightly condenses the 'Requirements' section by removing the specific examples of 'Methicillin susceptible' in the requirement list, the behavioral instruction and clinical criteria are functionally identical.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements explicitly listed 'Methicillin susceptible' and 'Methicillin resistant' as separate documentation examples -> October 2025 (New Version): Requirements generalized to 'the specific organism' while retaining the same code assignment logic.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Behaviors listed three distinct points including 'Recognize colonization as the presence of bacteria without necessarily causing illness or a specific disease process' -> October 2025 (New Version): Behaviors listed three points with the third point shortened to 'Recognize colonization as the presence of bacteria without necessarily causing illness'.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same text and behavioral rules for Code Title Note Definitions. The notes are described as appearing immediately under three-character code titles to define or provide examples of the category content in both versions. There are no alterations to the coding logic, placement, or illustrative purpose of these notes between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Notes appear immediately under a three-character code title to further define, or give examples of, the content of the category. -> October 2025 (New Version): Notes appear immediately under a three-character code title to further define, or give examples of, the content of the category.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines are functionally identical regarding the assignment of code R40.20 for unknown causes or undocumented scales. Both versions strictly maintain the prohibition against reporting unspecified coma codes or Glasgow Coma Scale scores for patients in medically induced comas or under sedation. The extracted behaviors and requirements lists are verbatim matches between the two versions, indicating a copy-paste update rather than a clinical revision.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code R40.20 when the underlying cause is unknown or scale is undocumented; prohibit for induced/sedated patients -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code R40.20 when the underlying cause is unknown or scale is undocumented; prohibit for induced/sedated patients
October 1, 2024 (Older): Do not report individual or total Glasgow coma scale scores for sedated patients -> October 2025 (New Version): Do not report individual or total Glasgow coma scale scores for sedated patients

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain identical definitions for the 'see' and 'see also' instructions within the Alphabetic Index. The raw text excerpts are character-for-character the same, indicating no update to the coding logic occurred between these versions. The feature remains a static navigational rule where 'see' is mandatory and 'see also' is optional.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): The 'see' instruction requires navigating to the referenced term, while 'see also' is optional if the original term suffices. -> October 2025 (New Version): The 'see' instruction requires navigating to the referenced term, while 'see also' is optional if the original term suffices.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly required documentation by clinicians other than the patient's provider for socioeconomic and psychosocial hazards. The October 2025 version retains this exact requirement without altering the logic, scope, or specific wording of the rule. The only variation is a minor grammatical adjustment in the extracted profile text ('and' vs 'or') which does not reflect a substantive change in the coding guideline itself.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Documentation must be provided by clinicians other than the patient's provider for socioeconomic and psychosocial circumstances. -> October 2025 (New Version): Documentation must be provided by a clinician other than the patient's provider for socioeconomic or psychosocial circumstances.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline mandates coding to the highest level of specificity when supported by the medical record. The October 2025 version retains this exact instruction without altering the logic, requirements, or raw text excerpt. There is no substantive delta between the two versions for this specific feature.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code to the highest level of specificity when supported by the medical record documentation. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code to the highest level of specificity when supported by the medical record documentation.

findings.section: Factors Influencing Health Status and Contact
findings.summary: The older version instructed coders to code these factors when documented as such, while the newer version adds the specific qualifier 'as the reason for encounter or service' to the behavior description. The requirements remain virtually identical, merely shifting from 'explicitly documented' to 'documented'. The raw text excerpts and taxonomy descriptions are identical, indicating the text was likely updated for formatting or minor wording without altering the underlying coding logic.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code factors influencing health status and contact with health services when documented as such. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code factors influencing health status and contact with health services when documented as the reason for encounter or service.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Condition must be explicitly documented as a factor influencing health status or contact with health services. -> October 2025 (New Version): Condition must be documented as a factor influencing health status or contact with health services.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions present identical requirements for coding factors influencing health status related to prophylactic organ removal. The only variation is a minor wording adjustment in the requirements section ('procedures' added in the new version), which does not alter the underlying coding logic or scope. Both versions mandate that conditions be explicitly documented and linked to Section I.C. 21.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): The condition must be related to prophylactic organ removal. -> October 2025 (New Version): The condition must be related to prophylactic organ removal procedures.
